Round 1: Use fighters and tactical_bombers from Japan, Korea, Manchuria, Kiangsu, and Formosa to support key land battles (Hunan, Yunnan, Kweichow) while keeping at least 2 fighters in range to defend the main fleet in 6 Sea Zone.
Round 1-2 Purchases: Emphasize 1-2 transports and 3-5 ground units (infantry/artillery) in Japan to sustain a steady flow of reinforcements to China and Southeast Asia.

Round 2: Finish off remaining Chinese pockets in Hunan/Kweichow/Yunnan and push into Anhwe/Chahar as needed to complete the buffer, avoiding deep pushes into Szechwan/Shensi/Suiyuan unless tactically necessary.
Maintain at least 6-8 ground units along the Kiangsu–Kiangsi–Kwangsi line to deter Chinese counterattacks and protect staging for the southern drive.
strategic_plans.expected_outcome: By end of Round 3, Japan controls all Chinese coastal territories (Kiangsu, Kiangsi, Kwangsi, Shantung) and the key inland buffer of Hunan, Yunnan, and Anhwe, with Chinese forces compressed to Szechwan/Shensi/Suiyuan and unable to threaten the coast.

strategic_plans.name: Prepare for Capture of Kwangtung and French Indo China
strategic_plans.reason: Kwangtung and French Indo China are the land bridge to Burma and India. Early preparation ensures a timely southern offensive once Japan is at war with the UK, preventing India from becoming an impregnable fortress.
actions: Round 1 Purchases: Buy at least 1 additional transport (preferably 2 total new transports this round if PUs allow) and 3-4 ground units in Japan to ensure sufficient lift capacity for a southern push.

Round 1: Concentrate the main fleet in 6 Sea Zone or reposition elements toward 20/36 Sea Zones while keeping it safe from Allied counterattacks, ensuring naval cover for future landings on Kwangtung and French Indo China.
Round 1-2: Move infantry and artillery from Japan, Korea, and Manchuria toward coastal staging points (Kiangsu, Kiangsi, Kwangsi) so they can be loaded quickly once war with the UK begins.

Round 2-3: Once politically allowed to attack the UK, conduct an amphibious assault on Kwangtung from 20 Sea Zone using transports loaded from Kiangsi/Kwangsi/Japan, with air support from Formosa, Kiangsu, and carriers.
Immediately after securing Kwangtung, shift transports and ground units to take French Indo China from Kwangsi/Siam, using Siamese infantry as follow-up garrison.
strategic_plans.expected_outcome: By end of Round 4, Japan has captured Kwangtung and French Indo China, with at least 5 ground units (infantry/artillery/mech_infantry/armour) stationed in French Indo China and Siam as a staging area toward Burma and India.

strategic_plans.name: Capture India and Cripple British Pacific
strategic_plans.reason: India is the British Pacific industrial hub. Its capture removes a major Allied production center, secures Japan’s western flank, and opens options into the Middle East and Africa while denying the Allies a key airbase and naval base.
actions: Rounds 2-4: Build up a strong ground stack (6-10 units including infantry, artillery, and possibly armour/mech_infantry) in French Indo China and Siam, supported by fighters and tactical_bombers on nearby carriers or airbases.
Rounds 3-5: Use transports in 35/37 Sea Zones to shuttle reinforcements from Japan and China to French Indo China, Malaya, and Burma, ensuring a continuous flow of troops toward the Indian front.
Once French Indo China and Malaya are secure, advance into Burma with a combined-arms force supported by air units from carriers and mainland airbases, aiming to clear Burma in one decisive battle.

From Burma, launch a coordinated attack on India when odds are clearly favourable: at least 6-8 attacking ground units plus 4-6 supporting air units, while ensuring the Japanese fleet can cover the Bay of Bengal (39 Sea Zone) against Allied naval interference.
After capturing India, immediately land follow-up infantry and station fighters/tactical_bombers there to hold against any Allied counterattack from the Middle East or sea.
strategic_plans.expected_outcome: By end of Round 6-7, Japan has captured India and holds Burma, Malaya, French Indo China, and Kwangtung, with India functioning as a secure forward base and British Pacific production eliminated.

strategic_plans.name: Secure Home Islands and Central Pacific Fleet Position
strategic_plans.reason: Japan’s offensive plans depend on a safe, concentrated fleet. Losing carriers or transports early would cripple the China and India campaigns and invite Allied counterattacks on the home islands and key staging areas.
actions: Round 1: Keep the main fleet concentrated in 6 Sea Zone or another safe sea zone out of range of overwhelming Allied air/naval strikes, while still projecting power toward China and the South Pacific.
Round 1 Purchases: Ensure at least one additional transport and consider a destroyer or submarine if Allied naval threats (US/ANZAC) are likely to contest nearby sea zones early.
Maintain at least 2-3 fighters and 1-2 tactical_bombers on carriers in the main fleet to provide strong air cover and strike capability against enemy ships.
Avoid overextending single carriers or small detachments into Allied air range (e.g., near the Philippines or Hawaii) without overwhelming force and air cover.
Use submarines and destroyers to screen the main fleet and threaten Allied shipping lanes, while always preserving enough naval strength to deter direct attacks on Japan and Korea.
strategic_plans.expected_outcome: Throughout Rounds 1-4, Japan maintains an intact, concentrated main fleet (2 carriers, 2 battleships, multiple escorts, and growing transport capacity) in the central-western Pacific (6/19/20 Sea Zones), ensuring secure reinforcement routes to China and Southeast Asia and deterring Allied aggression against the home islands.

reviews.plan_name: Secure Chinese Coast and Inland Buffer
reviews.actual_outcome: By end of Round 6, Japan had a strong stack in Kiangsu and some reinforcement in Kiangsi, but there is no evidence that Hunan, Yunnan, or Anhwe were taken, nor that all Chinese coastal territories were secured. Rounds 2-3 saw no territorial gains, and the first major set of battles only occurred in Round 4. Chinese forces were not clearly compressed into the interior by Round 3 as planned.
reviews.failure_chain:
Expected outcome by Round 3: control Kiangsu, Kiangsi, Kwangsi, Shantung plus Hunan, Yunnan, Anhwe, with China pushed back to Szechwan/Shensi/Suiyuan.
← Rounds 1-3: purchases focused on armour and only 1 transport, with minimal or unspecified land attacks; no explicit captures of Hunan/Yunnan/Anhwe are logged.
← Round 1: instead of coordinated multi-territory attacks on Hunan/Kweichow/Yunnan with air support, Japan conducted only a single unspecified battle, leaving the Chinese southern line largely intact.
← Rounds 2-3: Japan executed only "strategic troop movements" with no attacks or captures, allowing China to retain its interior and possibly reinforce.
← Force allocation and timing prioritized building tanks in Japan over early infantry/artillery mass on the China front, delaying the inland buffer push beyond the Round 3 target window.
reviews.root_cause_detail: Japan did not launch the planned coordinated Round 1–2 offensives into Hunan and Yunnan, and instead delayed major land operations until Round 4, missing the timing window to secure the inland buffer by Round 3.
reviews.lesson: For a China containment plan, Japan must attack Hunan and Yunnan no later than Round 2 with sufficient ground (3–4 infantry plus artillery) and air support from Japan/Korea/Manchuria. Skipping these early pushes and waiting until Round 4 allows China to maintain a viable interior and keeps the coastal buffer incomplete.
reviews.national_strategy_update: Update sp_secure_chinese_coast: add explicit prerequisite actions: (1) Round 1: attack Hunan OR Yunnan if odds ≥ 65%; (2) Round 2: ensure at least one of Hunan/Yunnan is captured and reinforced with ≥ 4 ground units. Add known_risk: "If Hunan and Yunnan are not taken by end of Round 2, compressing China by Round 3 becomes unlikely; de-prioritize India timetable accordingly."

reviews.plan_name: Prepare for Capture of Kwangtung and French Indo China
reviews.actual_outcome: By end of Round 6, Kwangtung and French Indo China do not appear to be under Japanese control. The main ground mass is still in Kiangsu/Kiangsi, and transports are only just being organized in SZ 6 with infantry loaded but not yet moved forward. No amphibious assaults on Kwangtung or FIC are recorded by Round 4, the target date.
reviews.failure_chain:
Expected outcome by Round 4: Kwangtung and FIC captured, with ≥ 5 ground units in FIC/Siam as staging.
← No logged amphibious assaults on Kwangtung or FIC in Rounds 2–4; first major battles are in Round 4 but not clearly against these targets.
← Transports: only 1 new transport by Round 2 and 1 by Round 3; the main transport mass (2 more) arrives Round 4, and another in Rounds 5–6, so lift capacity for a strong early southern push was delayed.
← Round 6 NCM shows 3 transports in SZ 6 with 6 infantry loaded but doctrine prevented optimal armour+infantry loading and forward movement to SZ 20; transports remained in SZ 6 instead of staging in SZ 20/36.
← Ground units: many tanks and infantry were built in Japan but only gradually moved south; Kiangsu/Kiangsi stacks were still forming in Round 6, indicating that earlier rounds did not prioritize staging to Kwangsi/20 SZ.
← Overall, the southern amphibious timetable slipped because transport purchases and forward positioning lagged behind the plan, and no decisive commitment to Kwangtung/FIC was made by Round 4.
reviews.root_cause_detail: Japan failed to purchase and forward-position enough transports by Rounds 1–2 and did not execute the planned Round 2–3 amphibious assault on Kwangtung, leaving the Kwangtung/FIC sequence unstarted by the Round 4 deadline.
reviews.lesson: When planning an early Kwangtung/FIC push, Japan must have at least 3–4 transports in SZ 6 by end of Round 2 and must move them to SZ 20 with loaded ground units by Round 3. If transports are still in SZ 6 and not staged forward by Round 3, the Kwangtung/FIC timetable should be considered failed and the India plan delayed or revised.
reviews.national_strategy_update: Update sp_prepare_kwangtung_fic: add quantitative milestones: (1) End Round 1: ≥ 2 transports in SZ 6; (2) End Round 2: ≥ 3 transports, with ≥ 4 ground units staged in Kiangsu/Kiangsi/Kwangsi; (3) Round 3: attempt Kwangtung assault if odds ≥ 70%. Add known_risk: "If transports are not in SZ 20 by Round 3, FIC capture by Round 4 is unlikely; downgrade India capture plan confidence."

reviews.plan_name: Capture India and Cripple British Pacific
reviews.actual_outcome: By end of Round 6, India has not been attacked or captured. Kwangtung and FIC are not clearly in Japanese hands, and there is no established ground stack in FIC/Siam/Burma. Japanese forces are concentrated in Kiangsu/Kiangsi and on transports in SZ 6, indicating that the India offensive is still in its preparatory phase rather than approaching execution.
reviews.failure_chain:
Expected outcome: India captured by Round 6–7, with Kwangtung, FIC, Malaya, and Burma already secured.
← By Round 6, Kwangtung and FIC are not confirmed captured; Burma and Malaya are not mentioned as Japanese holdings, so the land bridge to India is incomplete.
← The planned ground stack of 6–10 units in FIC/Siam by Rounds 2–4 never materialized; instead, large forces remained in Kiangsu and only began moving south in Round 6.
← Transport shuttling via SZ 35/37 never started; transports are still in SZ 6 with infantry loaded but not moved forward due to doctrine constraints and late staging.
← The prerequisite plan (sp_prepare_kwangtung_fic) slipped: no early Kwangtung/FIC capture, so the India timetable lost its foundation.
← Strategic focus and purchases emphasized armour in Japan without synchronizing transport capacity and forward staging, leaving offensive power stranded far from the India front within the 6-round window.
reviews.root_cause_detail: The India capture plan assumed an aggressive early timetable (India by Round 6–7) without enforcing prerequisite milestones for Kwangtung/FIC/Burma and transport staging. When those prerequisites slipped in Rounds 2–4, the plan was not formally downgraded or delayed, leaving an unrealistic expectation of early India capture.
reviews.lesson: India capture should be treated as a second-phase objective that only becomes active once Kwangtung and FIC are secured and a 6–10 unit stack exists in FIC/Siam. If Kwangtung and FIC are not both under Japanese control by end of Round 4, the target date for India must be pushed back and resources reallocated instead of continuing to assume a Round 6–7 capture.
reviews.national_strategy_update: Update sp_capture_india: add explicit dependencies: (1) Kwangtung and FIC must be controlled by end of Round 4; (2) ≥ 6 ground units in FIC/Siam by end of Round 4; (3) ≥ 3 transports operating in SZ 35/37 by Round 5. Add rule: "If these conditions are not met, automatically shift India target round to 8–9 and reduce plan confidence, freeing purchases for fleet defense or China."

reviews.plan_name: Secure Home Islands and Central Pacific Fleet Position
reviews.actual_outcome: Throughout Rounds 1–6, Japan maintained a concentrated main fleet, with carriers, battleships, escorts, and growing transport capacity. The main fleet is reported in SZ 20 with carrier-based air, and SZ 6 holds multiple transports. There is no indication of major Japanese naval losses or Allied strikes on the home islands; Japan’s IPC income is stable or slightly increasing (+5 in Round 6).
reviews.root_cause_detail: N/A — the plan’s expected outcome (intact, concentrated fleet in central-western Pacific) was achieved. Minor execution friction occurred with transport doctrine (inability to move empty transports forward), but this did not compromise fleet safety.
reviews.lesson: Keeping the main combat fleet concentrated in SZ 20 while using SZ 6 as a protected transport base effectively secures Japan’s reinforcement routes and deters Allied aggression. However, transport doctrine (requiring armour+infantry loading and forbidding empty forward moves) must be integrated into planning so that logistics do not lag behind fleet safety.
reviews.national_strategy_update: For sp_secure_home_fleet, add a coordination note: "When main fleet is in SZ 20, plan transport cycles so that loaded transports (1 armour + 1 infantry) move from SZ 6 to SZ 20 by noncombat, avoiding empty forward moves that doctrine forbids." Also add a success tag increasing confidence in concentrated-fleet posture around SZ 20 for early-mid game.

reviews.actual_outcome: This late-added plan appears intended to push south toward Burma around Round 4. By Round 6, Japanese forces are still massed in Kiangsu/Kiangsi, with no explicit captures of Burma, FIC, or Malaya. The Burma axis has not been opened; instead, Japan is still in the staging phase on the China coast.
reviews.failure_chain:
Expected outcome (inferred): establish a southern axis through FIC/Siam toward Burma by Round 7.
← By Round 6, no mention of Japanese control of FIC, Siam buildup, or any attack on Burma; the only detailed movements are north-China units shifting to Kiangsu/Kiangsi.
← The prerequisite capture of Kwangtung/FIC (handled by sp_prepare_kwangtung_fic) did not occur by Round 4, so the Burma axis had no starting base.
← Transport logistics remained behind schedule; transports were still in SZ 6 and not cycling through SZ 35/37 to land forces in Malaya/Burma.
← Because this plan was added at Round 4, it inherited already-slipped prerequisites and had insufficient time and resources to establish a credible Burma thrust by Round 6.
reviews.root_cause_detail: The Burma axis plan was activated in Round 4 without verifying that its prerequisites (Kwangtung/FIC capture and transport staging) were in place, making its Round 7 target unrealistic given the delayed southern expansion.
reviews.lesson: New mid-game plans (like a Burma axis) must be conditioned on the current board state. If Kwangtung and FIC are not yet secured by the time the plan is proposed, the Burma timetable must be extended or the plan shelved, rather than assuming an idealized progression from Round 4 onward.
reviews.national_strategy_update: Refine sp_r4_south_axis_burma: explicitly require that Kwangtung and FIC are controlled and that at least 2 transports can land units in FIC/Siam before the plan is activated. Add a rule: "Do not instantiate this plan at Round 4 if FIC is still Allied-controlled; instead, reassess for a later Burma push or alternative objectives."

reviews.actual_outcome: This plan was only activated at Round 6, with no specific actions or outcomes yet. By Round 6, Japan’s main fleet is in SZ 20 and transports are still organizing in SZ 6; there is no evidence of pressure on ANZAC territories (e.g., New Guinea, Queensland, New South Wales). The plan is effectively in its infancy and has not produced results within the 6-round window.
reviews.failure_chain:
Expected outcome (inferred): increase pressure on ANZAC by Round 9.
← Plan activated only at Round 6, leaving no time within the 6-round game window to execute meaningful operations.
← Fleet and transports are positioned more for China/India staging (SZ 20 and SZ 6) than for a southward lunge toward ANZAC.
← Earlier strategic focus on India/China left no dedicated resources for an ANZAC pressure line until very late, so this plan is more aspirational than actionable in the current timeframe.
reviews.root_cause_detail: The ANZAC pressure plan was introduced too late relative to the game’s 6-round evaluation window and without dedicated resources or fleet repositioning, making it impossible to assess or achieve its goals in time.
reviews.lesson: If ANZAC pressure is part of the overall Pacific strategy, it must be planned from the early game with explicit fleet and transport allocations (e.g., a carrier group and 1–2 transports earmarked for the south). Adding ANZAC pressure as a new plan in Round 6 without prior preparation will not yield results within a short horizon.
reviews.national_strategy_update: Update sp_pacific_pressure_ANZAC: add an activation condition: "Only instantiate this plan if, by Round 3, the main fleet or a detached task force can reach SZ 54/62 within 2 rounds and at least 1–2 transports are available for landings on ANZAC-adjacent islands." Also add a note that if India capture is the primary objective, ANZAC pressure should be secondary and only activated once India prerequisites are met.

reviews.actual_outcome: This air supremacy plan for the India front was activated at Round 6, but by that time Japan had not yet established a land front near India (no FIC/Burma control). Air units remained largely on carriers in SZ 20 or in Japan, with no redeployment toward an India-adjacent airbase. Thus, no air superiority over the India front was established within the 6-round window.
reviews.failure_chain:
Expected outcome (inferred): achieve air superiority over the India front by around Round 9.
← By Round 6, there is no Japanese land presence in FIC/Burma/Malaya to host fighters and tactical bombers near India.
← The India ground offensive (sp_capture_india) and southern staging (sp_prepare_kwangtung_fic) were behind schedule, so there was no defined "India front" to support.
← Air units were not repositioned in Round 6; they remained on carriers in SZ 20 or in Japan, reflecting the lack of a concrete ground axis toward India.
← The plan was added reactively at Round 6 rather than being integrated from earlier rounds with coordinated ground and naval movements.
reviews.root_cause_detail: The air supremacy plan was activated without a corresponding ground front or forward bases near India, making its objectives unattainable in the short term and misaligned with the actual board position.
reviews.lesson: Air supremacy plans must be tied to specific forward bases (e.g., FIC, Malaya, Burma) and activated only once those territories are secured or realistically within reach. Declaring an "India front" air plan while still fighting around Kiangsu/Kiangsi leads to misallocated planning with no actionable moves.
reviews.national_strategy_update: Refine sp_air_supremacy_india_front: add prerequisites: (1) Japan controls at least one of FIC, Malaya, or Burma; (2) at least 4 fighters/tacs are available to redeploy within 1 round to that base. Until these are met, do not activate the plan; instead, keep air units under generic fleet/home defense plans.
